heading: NEL RAGGIUNGIMENTO DELLA BEATITUDINE
card_summary: This chunk opens the final movement of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi with a meditation on beatitude as the ultimate harmonic resolution. Zorzi frames the celestial Jerusalem — described by Ezekiel and John of Patmos — as a cosmic musical instrument, a "celestial cithara" whose twenty modes correspond to the twenty steps of the medieval Guidonian hand. He invokes Augustine on the hidden numerical harmonies of glorified bodies, Pseudo-Dionysius on the Trinity as divine organ (Father as mover, Son as the organ itself, Spirit as the breath filling its pipes), and Isaiah and Paul on the ineffability of heavenly mysteries. John the Evangelist, described as resting on the "Parnassus" of Christ's breast, is presented as the supreme musical prophet of the supramundane continuum.
essay:
## The Final Canticle: Harmonia as Eschatological Fulfilment

Chunk 101 inaugurates the concluding section of the *Cantico Terzo* (Tono Ottavo), framed programmatically as the last and sweetest melody in a vast musical architecture. Zorzi opens with the metaphor of the master musician who, constrained by time and fatigue, nevertheless strives to render his final melody the most beautiful — an analogy for the "sommo Maestro delle muse" (the supreme Master of the Muses, i.e., God) who both distributes his melodies through time and draws them back into eternity.

## Time, Eternity, and the Architecture of Redemption

The central tension introduced is between temporal succession and eternal permanence. Zorzi appeals to Ecclesiastes ("la terra rimane in eterno") and the Psalms (the heavens will pass away but their movement will cease) to establish that the created order is simultaneously subject to time and liberated from it. The "melodia dolcissima della redenzione" was accomplished in a few days yet its fruit is eternal — a compression of Christological soteriology into musical terms.

> Egli compì la melodia dolcissima della redenzione non dirò in pochi mesi ma in pochi giorni e tuttavia presso di lui un'abbondante redenzione resta in perpetuo come rimane il suo frutto eterno.

<cite>p. 2445</cite>

Christ is here described as a harmonic force exceeding Orpheus and Amphion — the two paradigmatic musicians of antiquity whose music moved stones and tamed beasts. Christ's attraction of souls to himself after the Resurrection ("dopo essere stato innalzato da terra, avrà attirato a sé") is glossed as a superior *vis harmonica* that assigns each soul to its perfectly corresponding dwelling.

## Augustine and the Glorified Body's Hidden Harmonies

Zorzi invokes Augustine — "rapito talvolta da quella stessa dolcezza" — on the eschatological unveiling of numerical ratios and bodily harmonies (*numeri et harmoniae corporeae nunc latentes*) currently hidden in the flesh. In the beatific state these latent harmonies will resound together with all other singers in public praise of the Artificer and his supreme beauty. This is a significant claim in Zorzi's cosmological project: the body itself encodes harmonic ratios that will become audible only at the resurrection.

> credimus etiam numeri, et harmoniae corporeae nunc latentes simul cum aliis concentoribus in publicas laudes Artificis, et summae illius pulchritudinis circumsonare videbuntur.

<cite>p. 2446 (Latin column)</cite>

## Ezekiel and John as Celestial Scribes

Zorzi identifies Ezekiel and John the Evangelist as the two "scribi celesti" (celestial scribes) of the two Testaments who have described the heavenly dwelling places with "figure perfettamente esatte e misure e suddivisioni del tutto armoniose" under the teaching of the Holy Spirit. He distinguishes their respective competencies: Ezekiel provides architectural measurements, tribal disposition, sacrifices, and the name of the city; John, as "amico intimo del Principe e ammesso ai suoi sacri penetrali," sings sweeter and more interior canticles.

Both visionaries, however, are shrouded in *caligo* (dense fog) — a term with Dionysian resonance for the apophatic darkness surrounding divine realities. Zorzi invokes the classic Pauline-Isaianic formula of ineffability ("occhio non vide, né orecchio udì, né mai entrarono nel cuore dell'uomo") to explain why these scriptural accounts remain opaque without divine illumination.

> Uterque tamen ea caligine obnubilati, ut nisi supremus sol calore suo illius crassitudinem extenuet, et sua luce eiusdem tenebras expellat, nec audire quidem quod dicunt, nec videre quod describunt, et metiuntur, aliquatenus poterimus.

<cite>p. 2447 (Latin column)</cite>

The threefold reason for scriptural obscurity is a notable scholastic construction: (1) enigmatic sensible figures are necessary for the soul to ascend from known to unknown; (2) adequate language for heavenly realities has not yet been given to any human speaker or hearer; (3) the mind weighed down by corporeal mass cannot ascend to these heights (citing Isaiah 64:4 and 1 Corinthians 2:9).

## John of Patmos as Supreme Musical Prophet

Zorzi then pivots to a rapturous account of John's prophetic authority. John is described as resting on the "Parnaso" (*pegaseo pectoris Christi* in the Latin) of Christ's breast — the Johannine topos of the beloved disciple reclining at the Last Supper — between the "two peaks" (*duo cacumina*) of the divine and human natures. This image merges Neoplatonic mountain symbolism with Christological two-natures theology. From this position John was "invaso da una tale luce e ricolmato da una tale ispirazione entusiastica" that he became a prophet (*vates*) of divine realities.

> nunc ab angelo, nunc a supremo concentore Christo Iesu, nunc a diversis circumsonantibus edoctus, tradidit nobis illa consummata cantica, mirandoque artificio hinc inde discurrens, nunc conscendendo, nunc descendendo, plenis musicae legibus, nullum modulum supramundani instrumenti, nullamque vocem musicae distributionis reliquit intactam.

<cite>p. 2448 (Latin column)</cite>

## The Twenty Modes and the Guidonian Hand

A striking technical claim follows: Zorzi maps John's Apocalypse onto the twenty steps (*gradus*) of the Guidonian hand — the medieval pedagogical device for solmization and sight-reading that assigns the gamut's twenty pitches to joints of the left hand. Zorzi asserts that John's Revelation touches exactly twenty *moduli* in which the entire supracelestial harmony is consummated. The heavenly Jerusalem is figured as a celestial cithara (*cithara coelestis*) on which all twenty voices are tuned and played by the *summus Archimusaeus* (supreme Arch-Musician).

## Pseudo-Dionysius: The Trinitarian Organ

The chunk closes with an explicit citation of Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite on the Trinity as a musical instrument. The Father is the primal Mover; the Son is "constituted as an organ of the Father" (*organum Dei factum est*), with all the ideas (*ideae*) of created things existing in him as pipes (*fistulae*); the Spirit is the breath (*flatus*) that fills everything, citing Wisdom 1:7 ("Lo spirito del Signore ha ricolmato la terra"). The organic/pneumatic metaphor is complete: Father as player, Son as the instrument's body and pipes, Spirit as the animating breath.

> Gaudent omnes (inquit Dionysius) movente Patre, Filius namque organum Dei factum est, cum in ipso rerum omnium fabricatarum ideae existant, et in ipso omnes tamquam fistulae collocatae sint.

<cite>p. 2449 (Latin column)</cite>

This Trinitarian organology is the theological climax of Zorzi's entire harmonic cosmology: the universe is literally an instrument played by the Trinity, and beatitude consists in being fully integrated into its resonance. The program for the remainder of this final section is announced: following John's Apocalypse, and coordinating it with Ezekiel, Zorzi will expound the twenty modules of the supramundane harmony, each corresponding to a chord in the celestial cithara of the New Jerusalem.
